pulse: <cite index="2-4,2-5,2-6">U.S. equities closed at record highs on Friday, with the S&P 500 climbing 0.22% to 7,580.06 and the Nasdaq Composite settling up 0.2% at 26,972.62</cite>. <cite index="1-2">Investors were encouraged by optimism around artificial intelligence (AI)-driven tech growth and hopes of a 60-day U.S.-Iran ceasefire extension</cite>.

why_paras: <cite index="10-2,10-3">Concentrated strength in large-cap technology stocks has been the undeniable engine behind Wall Street's record-breaking trajectory, with the technology sector within the S&P 500 surging more than 15% throughout May</cite>. <cite index="2-9,13-13">Dell Technologies surged nearly 33% after reporting Q1 earnings that rocketed past expectations, with the company announcing a $51 billion backlog for its AI server unit</cite>, triggering sympathy rallies across infrastructure peers.

<cite index="1-2">Optimism around artificial intelligence (AI)-driven tech growth and hopes of a 60-day U.S.-Iran ceasefire extension offset concerns over high inflation</cite>. <cite index="10-2,10-3,10-4,10-5">Concentrated performance in technology created a divergence, as a majority of non-technology cyclical sectors experienced modest pullbacks over the same 30-day period, yet these massive technology enterprises carry immense market capitalization weight to drag the broader indexes into uncharted territory</cite>.

The market finish validates a bifurcated narrative: mega-cap tech and AI infrastructure beneficiaries are rewarded, but breadth is narrowing (only 2 of 11 sectors green on Friday). <cite index="10-9">The long-term sustainability of this tech-led equity bull market will depend heavily on whether structural inflation begins to moderate in response to cooling GDP growth, or whether consumer spending patterns finally fracture under the weight of sustained high interest rates and elevated energy costs</cite>. For a Singapore retail investor, this concentration in US mega-cap growth is a significant portfolio risk—rotation into defensive or international equity exposure merits consideration.
macro: <cite index="1-2">AI infrastructure buildout and geopolitical de-escalation (U.S.-Iran ceasefire hopes) are offsetting sticky inflation concerns</cite>; the market is willing to look past near-term macro headwinds if corporate earnings remain resilient.

<cite index="17-10,17-11">Profit margins reached a record 13.4% in Q1 2026, with IT sector margins at 29.1% (up from 25.4% a year ago)</cite>, indicating that productivity gains and pricing power are sustaining earnings even in a high-rate environment.

<cite index="17-21">The forward 12-month P/E for the S&P 500 stood at 20.9 by late April, above both the five-year average of 19.9 and ten-year average of 18.9</cite>, signaling that the rally has priced in a continuation of strong growth; any deceleration will hurt valuations.
opportunity_radar:
• AI Infrastructure Beneficiaries — Dell's blowout Q1 earnings with 88% revenue growth and 214% EPS growth, plus a $51 billion backlog for AI servers, validate a 'tide that lifts all boats' narrative for infrastructure peers. Memory and server suppliers remain in early innings of a multi-year capex cycle. (e.g. MU, AVGO, MSFT)
  • Margin Expansion via Pricing Power — Q1 earnings season provided substantive validation of the fundamental case for U.S. equities, particularly in the technology and industrial sectors, with strength that is genuine and broad-based. Profitability is real, not just multiple expansion. (e.g. IBM, MSFT, AVGO)
  • Defensive Tech Plays on Valuation — The market's reaction to megacap tech earnings revealed a shift: investors are now pricing AI capital spending against evidence of returns, not on the size of the commitment alone. Lower-beta names with proven AI ROI (e.g., Microsoft cloud, Google search) are re-rated as capital allocators. (e.g. MSFT, GOOGL)

HOLD CORE, REBALANCE SELECTIVELY. With VIX at a calm 15.74 and S&P 500 at all-time highs (+7.5% vs 50-day average), the environment favors staying invested; however, breadth (only 2 of 11 sectors green) and narrow concentration (tech +2.23%) signal rising concentration risk. Retail investors in Singapore should trim mega-cap technology overweights if they exceed 40% of equities, lock in profits on high-beta AI infrastructure plays (MU, QRVO near 52-week highs), and rotate into defensive large-caps (MSFT, AAPL, IBM) or value-tilted plays (Citigroup). Macro tail risks (inflation stickiness, rate persistence, geopolitical re-escalation) are real—reducing leverage and maintaining 5-10% cash is prudent at current valuations (forward P/E 20.9x).

stw.reason: Micron's +889.81% YoY surge reflects genuine structural demand from AI hyperscalers building HBM and DRAM infrastructure. With a $51B backlog visible across the industry and a beta of 2.68, MU is positioned for multi-year capex cycles; hold for 5 years to capture the full AI infrastructure supercycle.
stw.name: MU

stw.reason: Microsoft's +6.07% monthly gain and proven Azure/AI ROI justify its current 0.85 beta (lower volatility). With +5.45% daily strength, it's a core holding for 1-year stability; rebalance if it exceeds 15% of tech allocation, as valuations are now pricing in continued capex returns.
stw.name: MSFT

stw.reason: Qorvo's 28.6x P/E is stretched after 31.22% YoY gains; the -2.69% daily decline and 4.6% pullback from 52-week highs signal momentum exhaustion in RF semiconductors. Exit now to redeploy into AI infrastructure names with better structural tailwinds.
stw.name: QRVO

stw.reason: AMD's extraordinary 354.67% YoY return and continued momentum (+14.79% weekly, +53.1% monthly) reflect AI GPU demand and server market share gains. With a 2.69 beta, it offers leveraged exposure to compute infrastructure buildout; hold for 5 years as a core AI infrastructure play.
stw.name: AMD

stw.reason: <cite index="17-20">Meta fell roughly 9% after raising 2026 capital expenditure guidance to $125–145 billion, even as it beat on earnings</cite>. The market is scrutinizing capex-to-returns, so hold for 1 year to see if metaverse investments justify the spend; current -0.72% YoY return reflects this uncertainty.
